speaker: interviewer
text: Hyman Flancbaum, F-L-A-N-C-B-A-U-M, and this is  roll number three.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were waiting for this Polish fellow, and he didn't show up.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't show up. Yeah, he didn't show up. Then we decided we're gonna stay till night, another day. And at night, we're gonna go back to this fellow. And we'll-- we'll make a decision. Either we're going to leave him-- because we saw it's not right, he fooled me. We were cheated. And we have to face it that this is not the place to stay. We have to move. Because we-- you couldn't hang around too long in the place. Then we went back at night to the same people, to the same Polish family, and he didn't show up. He didn't show up. I say, look, Mrs. Sarzsinski. We were trying to talk rough, and soft, and all kinds of motifs together. She said, look. My husband, I don't know nothing what he promise you. In fact, she didn't know anything, because we dealt with him. Then I said, give me all  your breads, what she had. And she gave it to us, gladly. And she gave us, like, a thousand-- not dollar, thousand zlotys-- and we left the place, and we went away, because the same night we had to go-- move further up. And we-- we-- our-- we-- we-- we knew that-- that in-- in the woods is the Armia-- Armia Krajowa, Armia Krajowa, Armia Ludowa. Maybe we're going to join a partisan group. We didn't know where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you-- physically, where were you? Near what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near my town where I was born. This is-- this was between Volzhsk and [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, [INAUDIBLE] not [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. And we moved on, and we went night. When I was walking at night, I saw like a shadow that somebody is walking, too. And I said in Polish, [POLISH]. Come closer to me. I want to know who you are. Because we were afraid maybe somebody is there. Then he said-- then he recognized my voice. I said-- I said, [POLISH]. He said, [POLISH]. Well, he-- he was-- he-- he-- when he ran away two days before, we were sure that he was killed. Remember, I sung you the [INAUDIBLE] He were run away before. And I said-- and I asked, [INAUDIBLE] where did you-- and he was a very rich kid at home. They left a fortune in Europe, I mean in that little town. That he was the-- the richest family in town. Then he said, Hyman. His voice changed. His face changed. He was not-- he was not already the same. He said, Hyman, we made a mistake that we escaped. We have no place to go. Because I was already-- he went to his Polacks which they give him a fortune away. Everything was denied to him. Nothing was given to him. He said, now we have-- now we have to try something new. And we already five. And we walked, and we walked. We walked, and we stopped in the-- in the woods. A whole night, it was in the morning. We were tired already. Then they say they're gonna pick that time in the-- in the woods, was-- we-- we had bread but we had no water, and we had no nothing else. Then we-- then we had to go to a place. We had to find a little place where this is a little water. It wasn't clean water to drink, but just water. When we stopped in that little place, that was very early in the morning. Maybe 5 or 6 o'clock in the morning. Then two-- then those four went to pick blueberries. And I remained in this place. I said-- yes. I found a piece of paper-- excuse me-- which someone had-- excuse me a hundred times-- he wiped himself. Then I said, this piece of paper is from the factory where I used to work. I said, here must be people, which they're-- either my brother, because I'd lost my brother. Then he-- then those four went to pick blueberries. And I picked papers. And they said to them each other, see Hyman, he-- I'm already crazy. I went crazy. I went crazy. I don't know what the hell goes on with me. I'm picking papers, dirty pieces of papers, which is-- which is nothing. Then, while I was sitting there this morning, I heard talking. But I didn't know who was talking. And I heard someone, like, making, whoo. Somebody is washing themselves. Then slowly, slowly, slowly, I walked over. I walked over, and I saw my brother. When I saw my brother, I grabbed him and I started to hug him and kiss him. He said, Hyman! [SPEAKING POLISH] I said-- I made a statement. I said, David, I had a that feeling that you are here. Matter of fact, I found a piece of paper, which I gave a couple days before. This is-- this is the piece-- I took it with me, I didn't leave it. I took that piece of paper, took it and saved it in my pocket. And the guy which was with my brother, he used to give us soups in lager. Then we were already-- then I start yelling for the other people. I said, I found my brother. They thought I'm crazy. They didn't believe me. They came over, and we kissed each other and hugging-- because my brother was a very capable boy. Very bright boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were already seven people. We were already seven people. Then we-- we are trying to go. Where are we going to go? Then one said, you know what? We're gonna look for a place which is gonna be a few little trees, not too many trees, and we're gonna make ourselves a bunker. And we're going to stay here, we're going to live here, we're going to see what we have to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, a day-- or two days, I don't remember how long-- came another fellow. This fellow, which I have here a picture of him, and he was a tailor. He was older than me already, a few years older. Maybe 10 years older. We were very happy that we found him, and we are-- and we were trying. And my brother was very capable, too. And we were already eight. When we were already eight people, we were-- we-- we opened, like, a-- we built a bunker. We went in the-- in the little town-- not a town, a little country place-- and we bought a little stove to make hot water, to boil-- to cook potatoes, a little bit rice, a little bit to live. To go on. And then this guy used to take-- he said he's a tailor, and they-- and they-- and they were stealing those blankets from the Russian-- from the Germans. And we used to make coats for those people. Coats, pants, jackets, everything. And they gave us bread, they gave us potatoes. We didn't have to steal already, we-- we earned it. And we were there in that place. We were there in that place, and this was our life, livelihood. One-- I don't remember exactly when and when-- when, while we were walking, we found-- we met two also Jewish people. One a Russian, and one from our town, which-- which I used to go to his-- his father used to be my teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember I told you about Rosh Hashannah, [INAUDIBLE]? His father was praying. And his son became an officer in the Russian partisans. Then he said to us, why do you have to be on your own? Why don't you join us? Gladly! We accepted right away. Why not? Because, you know, it was a pleasure. And we joined this army, the Russian army-- the-- the Russian people. Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Frohme ?] Zelinger. When we-- when we joined those people, the first few days was like a honeymoon. It was beautiful. And we had to go work. We had to go make practicing how to shoot, how to do this, how to do this, how to do this. We were learning something. And the day went fast, it wasn't so dreary now and so on. But after awhile, they picked, let's say, two fellows-- you and Steve has to go today to this-and-this-- to this-and-this little town to put up a bomb on the-- the train will go through under bridge, to knock out the bridge. Those two people never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the group all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know how many. A lot. There was a lot of people. I would say maybe 200, or 300 people, I don't remember-- I don't know how much. We didn't know how much. It was already-- because a-- a little bit here, a little bit here, they were-- they were spread all over. We didn't know. We only knew-- only know, let's say, like 20, 30 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there women in this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few women, not too many. Few of them. Mostly men. Mostly men. When we were there, we talked to each other. We said, this is good-- it's good, and it's no good. Because in the next day, it's gonna be-- next day I got to go. We have to go. We-- this-- this-- this is a plan which everyone has to go. You cannot say, I am sick today, I don't go. If-- if your name was-- was called, you had to go. Was no excuse no. Then we decided-- we decided, while we were there, to escape from them. It wasn't so easy to escape from them, because they control the-- the whole-- the whole woods was controlled by those people. They controlled it, the whole thing. And this was-- no. Yes. While we were there in the partisan, was a big-- how you call, is a-- a raid of us. Through the Germans they came with horses, and with dogs, and with tanks, to the woods. They was trying to get us. This was the 28th of October. Was a miserable, cold day weather. Very bad day, very bad. And everyone escaped. Everyone runned on his own. And I run with my brother that day. And we knew that after that night-- after the night, we get back to our bunker, our place. That day was so-- snowing, freezing, and I found a little river with a tree, like somebody prepared for me. And I was with my brother under that, laying from the morning till 5 or 6 o'clock at night, in that river, at night. And when we woke up at night, both at like 6 o'clock, we wanted to go back to our place. We came back. Was only, we were eight. Was only four back. Two was missing. We didn't know what happened to those two people. After a few days later, we heard that they were shot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Either they were shot Russians, I mean by the-- by the Germans-- by whom, we don't know. Till now we don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the partisans Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they are not only Russian. They were Polish-- there were Polish groups, too. Was a lot of Polish groups. It was two groups. One were fighting the other. Was like a Communist group, and then it was a conservative group. Very conservative. They hated Jews terribly. They don't like Jews. We could never get along with-- with each other. [DRINKS] Then we went back that night, then we didn't go to this bunker. We left that bunker. We didn't go back to this bunker because we were afraid. Because we-- we had-- we saw-- we saw-- [INAUDIBLE]. We saw that somebody was already in this place. We didn't know who were there. Then we were afraid. We never-- we went back just to take out that little oven, but we had-- we had our-- we had-- we didn't have too much. We had only minor things there. And we pulled out that little stove, because this stove was a lifesaver for us. We went and we tried to dig another bunker, another place. While we were-- while we were looking for another place, we found other two Jewish people. We-- they joined us also, also from our little town. And we were back eight. More than eight, we had no place. Because we built a place only for eight. Then we went over, and we had one Polack which we trusted him. He knew all the time where we are. This guy, his name was [? Politch, ?] a Polack. Then-- then we-- then we made another little place, another hiding, and we were there maybe another month, or six weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This Polack came to us at night, like maybe 12 o'clock at night. And he came to us and he said, look. You have to leave this place, because we had-- we had a meeting today in church. And our-- the-- the-- the reverend talked about you and knew exactly the location that you are, and a few fellows will come and they're gonna kill you. He was so good to us. He took us to his house because we don't have nowhere to go. Very cold night. Very cold night. He took us to his house, and we slept over in his house. His wife was so good to us. I remember I was-- I couldn't warm up myself. Then she took a blanket and covered us. At night, she went down, that woman, and covered us. And in morning, we had to leave, because it was no place to be there. We couldn't stay with him, with those people. Because he would be killed and we would be killed. Then he gave us tools, because we had no more other-- the other tools was-- When they came in the same night, they bombed the whole bunker where we was. They-- they spoiled even that oven that we had. They demolished this, too. They-- they made everything-- nothing. We had nothing left over. Then this guy prepared us another little stove. Took us another few days. He did this for us. And we built another bunker. I am telling you the story so quick, but this took us days. It wasn't so easy to make it in a day. And-- and that-- this was already 1945. No, 1944. While we were there, while-- in-- in that-- then later, we went back to tailoring. This guy was-- was the-- the boss and we worked the whole day. And we made suits, and we made pants and jackets for the-- for the-- for those Polish people. The only guy which knew us is only this guy. We didn't trust any other guy. Everyone came to this [INAUDIBLE]-- to this Polack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked, what do you need, what do you want? And he brought us the work. He was like a broker to us. We didn't get paid. We only get paid food. And he get the money, whatever he wanted. We didn't know how much he took, what he took. Where were you getting the material from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the blankets, from the Russian blanket-- from, not the Russian, from the German blankets. Only from blankets. This was the material. And we were there, it was already rumors that the Russian try to cross the Vistula, the-- the-- how do you call that? They wanted to conquer the Germans again. And we were there from 1940-- yes. And, like, the end of December, we heard very big fighting that goes on. We didn't know where, because we were in the woods. And they were fighting already. The Germans and Russians start an offensive. They were fighting bitterly. Bitterly fight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they throw, how you call this? [INAUDIBLE] how you call this? Papers, that the Russian are coming. That we-- we're gonna liberate you soon. Be prepared. Be prepared that you're gonna to be free.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. We was happy, but we didn't trust nobody. We didn't-- we-- we couldn't trust nobody. The 12th-- I think the 12th of December, I don't remember exactly the date. I found a piece of paper with those-- like a paper, a big paper-- that we are close. We are-- we are closing-- we are closing up the Germans. We're gonna finish them up. And this took maybe a month. I don't know, a month exactly. By the 13th of January, they was conquered. But we didn't go out, because we were still afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 18th of January, I and my brother-- no, I and my brother, my brother said to me, you know what, Hyman? How long we gonna stay here? We-- we lost contact with that Polack already. We were afraid for the Polack because it was-- change-- the whole-- the whole situation changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we said, no. We gonna-- we gonna switch this place. We're gonna go away from here. And I went back to my little town when I was born. I went home to my house, where-- where we had our place. And we came over to this place, was Russian. Were already Russian army. We came in, was a Russian officer, a Jew. He said, look. I am happy that you lived through, but nobody likes us. Our place is Palestine. Nobody likes us. I want that you should know, I am a Russian in Russian army. I am fighting for the Russian fatherland. The only place is for us, go to Israel. I mean, not Israel, Palestine. That time it was Palestine. And he gave us 20 zlotys, each of them-- each of us. And don't stay in that little town, because this is no-- this is not safe for you. And if you have a chance, go to a big city. [DRINKS] We didn't-- we couldn't go so fast away to leave everything. What-- we wanted to see, maybe somebody's alive. Maybe somebody's still living. Then we fooled around two or three days. During those two or three days, came where we used to station, that room in that apartment, came in friends which I used to go to school with in Poland. They said, you I wouldn't kill, you I wouldn't kill. Don't worry about it, you can stay here. The same night, those same fellows which they said they wouldn't kill me, they wouldn't kill you, they killed two of our fellows. The same day. The same night. I was lucky to escape. Not to escape, I walked up on the-- on the roof and I-- and I-- I ran away to-- to another Polack. The next day-- morning, I came down, was two people dead already. Was another two people dead. Then my brother said to me, you know, Hyman, we have to leave this place too. Then we went to Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had a pogrom in 1942, you probably heard. In 19-- 1945, they had pogrom that killed about maybe 59 Jews. Was for Kielce, you-- you were reading about this? You read about this? In 1945. And-- and that time was the President Landau. He said, this is not the place for us, we have to move to Lodz. Even went to Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Lodz. We went to Lodz. We found ourself an apartment. And we start to live already, to work, to-- to do something. And everyone's start to-- start to live. I didn't have a profession. I didn't know what to do. And I was-- I was good in-- in doing-- how to do business. And I am trying to do business. I was trying to do businesses with my brother. I went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I went to do business. And I was trying to find my family. I was trying to go, let's say somebody told me that Bergen-Belsen was liberated. I said that we're gonna go to Bergen-Belsen I'm going to find somebody. I'm gonna go-- first I went to Prague, to Czechoslovakia. Over there was a Jewish community. I went there to the Jewish community, didn't find nobody. I went-- I went to Berlin, was also Jewish community. I didn't find nobody. There was people, but not my people. I was looking for my brothers and sisters. I went to Bergen-Belsen. I found, not-- not relatives, just friends, which I used to know them. And I communicate from Berlin to Bergen-Belsen. Because officially, I used to live in Berlin with my brother. And later, my brother find his wife-- his wife, and he married her. He married her in Lodz, in 1945, May the 12th. And I used to live out there with my brother. And he married my-- my-- he married his wife, and so I start off my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn that the rest of your family had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Perished?

speaker: interviewer
text: --not survived, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- to be honest with you, I don't know till now. But they told us that our-- this-- this transport went to-- to Treblinka. They always guessed. To say exactly when and what, no. I made myself a [INAUDIBLE] like [INAUDIBLE] days in HaShem, I have my [INAUDIBLE]. But to be-- I don't know. I don't know if anybody is out here. I don't know if they're all dead. Till now I am sure they're dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were commuting between Bergen-Belsen and Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Berlin, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]
